summary: Fawn Nguyen, a middle school math teacher, offers a practitioner's perspective on the Common Core State Standards, distinguishing between standards (ingredients), curriculum (the dish), and testing while defending the CCSS against critics who conflate these elements. She argues the standards are superior to previous state standards, bringing consistency, equity, and coherence to math education nationwide. Her main concern isn't the standards themselves but improper implementation and insufficient teacher training in mathematical content knowledge. She emphasizes that teaching math requires deep understanding of math, not just a college degree, and highlights the 8 Mathematical Practices as the most valuable aspect of CCSS for developing lifelong learning habits.
key_points: Standards, curriculum, and testing are related but distinct - blaming standards for bad curriculum or tests is like blaming ingredients for a poorly prepared dish
The biggest risk to CCSS success is improper implementation and lack of sustained professional development for teachers in content knowledge
Teaching elementary mathematics requires deep mathematical understanding, not just a college education - a perverse notion that anyone can teach basic math
Testing should help students learn and inform teaching, but over-testing takes time away from actual learning
The 8 Mathematical Practices are the most valuable part of CCSS, teaching lifelong habits applicable across all disciplines
money_quotes: We can't blame the ingredients when the dish is blandly prepared any more than we can blame the standards when the curriculum is poorly written.
It is a perverse notion to think that anyone with a college education can teach elementary mathematics. Teaching mathematics requires knowing mathematics.
To know a concept is to understand it backward and forward, inside and outside, to recognize it in different forms and structures.
My elementary teacher lied to me! She said we always get a smaller number when we divide two numbers.
Just because one can make tuna casserole does not make one a chef.
Testing is only good for kids when it helps them learn and become more successful.
I struggle with telling my kids that I value them and their learning, yet at the end of each quarter, the narrative sum of their learning is a letter grade.
We teachers do this very hard work – day in and day out, late into the nights and into the weekends – because we love our kids and we love teaching.
key_insight: The success or failure of Common Core math standards depends not on the standards themselves but on whether teachers receive adequate professional development in deep mathematical content knowledge to implement them properly.
